questions.question: Explain the difference between malloc() and calloc() with examples.
keyPoints: malloc allocates raw, uninitialized memory (contains garbage values)
calloc allocates zero-initialized memory (all bytes set to zero)
malloc takes single argument (total bytes), calloc takes two (number of elements and element size)
Syntax: malloc(n*sizeof(int)) vs calloc(n, sizeof(int))

questions.question: What is memory leak? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Memory leak occurs when allocated memory is not freed
Program loses reference to allocated memory
Prevention: always pair malloc with free, set pointer to NULL after freeing
Use tools like valgrind to detect leaks

questions.question: Write a C program to dynamically allocate a 1D array of n integers and initialize all elements to zero.
keyPoints: Use calloc(n, sizeof(int)) for zero-initialization
Alternative: malloc + loop initialization
Must check for NULL after allocation
Must free memory after use

questions.question: Explain the purpose of realloc() function with an example scenario.
keyPoints: realloc() resizes previously allocated memory block
Can expand or shrink existing allocation
May move data to new location if needed
Returns new pointer (always reassign, don't use old pointer)

questions.question: What is a dangling pointer? How can it be avoided?
keyPoints: Dangling pointer references freed memory
Accessing it causes undefined behavior
Avoid by setting pointer to NULL after free()
Avoid by ensuring no references to memory after freeing

questions.question: Explain the advantages of dynamic arrays over static arrays.
keyPoints: Size determined at runtime based on actual needs
Memory efficiency - no pre-allocation of unused space
Enables variable-sized data structures
Can be resized during program execution

questions.question: Describe the steps to create a 2D dynamic array in C using the pointer-to-pointer method.
keyPoints: Allocate array of pointers (rows): malloc(rows * sizeof(int*))
For each row, allocate array of integers: malloc(cols * sizeof(int))
Access elements using arr[i][j] syntax
Free in reverse order: free each row, then free row pointers

questions.question: Write a complete C program to implement a dynamic array that grows automatically when full. Include functions for insertion, display, and proper memory management.
keyPoints: Use struct with array pointer, size, and capacity
Initialize with initial capacity using malloc/calloc
When inserting, check if size equals capacity - if so, use realloc to double capacity
Ensure NULL check after realloc and proper cleanup with free
Time complexity considerations for resize operations

questions.question: Explain the concept of heap memory and stack memory. How does dynamic allocation use heap memory? Provide examples showing the differences.
keyPoints: Stack: automatic allocation/deallocation, fast, limited size, LIFO behavior
Heap: manual management, larger but slower, allocated via malloc/calloc
Dynamic arrays use heap - memory persists until explicitly freed
Stack variables: automatic lifetime (scope-based), Heap: explicit lifetime (programmer-controlled)

questions.question: What are the common problems in dynamic memory allocation? Explain with examples and solutions for each.
keyPoints: Memory leaks: not freeing memory - solution: always pair malloc/free
Dangling pointers: using freed memory - solution: set pointer NULL after free
NULL pointer dereference: not checking allocation failure - solution: always check return value
Memory fragmentation: solution: allocate appropriately sized blocks, use memory pools
Double free: freeing same memory twice - solution: set pointer to NULL after free

questions.question: Compare contiguous and non-contiguous memory allocation for 2D arrays. Explain with diagrams and code examples.
keyPoints: Contiguous: single malloc(rows*cols*sizeof(int)), calculate index manually: arr[i*cols + j]
Non-contiguous: array of pointers to rows, each row separate malloc
Contiguous: better cache performance, simpler single free
Non-contiguous: easier row operations, potential memory fragmentation
Trade-offs: memory usage, access patterns, cache locality
